1: revelation of God God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. The most complete revelation of this divine refuge is the Incarnation: God with us (Matthew 1:23 — Immanuel). Christ is the very present help — not distant but tabernacling among us (John 1:14). The church sings this psalm knowing the refuge is a person: in him all things hold together (Colossians 1:17).
2: shadow Therefore we will not fear though the earth gives way, though the mountains be moved into the heart of the sea, The earthquake at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51-54) and the cosmic signs of the Passion are the labor pains of the new creation. Those in Christ have no ultimate fear even when the earth gives way, because he has overcome the world (John 16:33).
3: shadow though its waters roar and foam, though the mountains tremble at its swelling. Chaos imagery reaching to Revelation (Revelation 17:15; Psalms 21:1 — the sea was no more). Christ commands the sea: he rebuked the wind and the raging waves, and they ceased (Luke 8:24). The trembling mountains give way to the unshakable kingdom (Hebrews 12:27-28).
4: direct There is a river whose streams make glad the city of God, the holy habitation of the Most High. John 7:38-39 identifies the river with the Spirit given through Christ: out of his heart will flow rivers of living water. Revelation 22:1-2 depicts the river of the water of life flowing from the throne of God and of the Lamb — Christ is the source of the river that makes glad the city of God.
5: shadow God is in the midst of her; she shall not be moved; God will help her when morning dawns. God is in the midst — fulfilled when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14). The morning dawning is the resurrection: early on the first day of the week (John 20:1) the dawn brought the help of God in the greatest act of history.
6: shadow The nations rage, the kingdoms totter; he utters his voice, the earth melts. Acts 4:25-26 quotes Psalms 2:1-2 (the raging nations) in the same idiom. Christ's voice melts opposition: at the name of Jesus every knee should bow (Philippians 2:10). The voice that spoke creation into being will speak the new creation at the parousia.
7: revelation of God The LORD of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress. With us — Immanuel (Matthew 1:23). The LORD of hosts who commands angelic armies is the one who chose not to call twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) in order to save his people. The fortress is not a wall of stone but the risen Christ in whom believers are hidden (Colossians 3:3).
8: shadow Come, behold the works of the LORD, how he has brought desolations on the earth. The call to behold God's desolating works — the Passion is the supreme desolation, the place of a skull (Golgotha). The angel's invitation reverses it: Come, see the place where he lay (Matthew 28:6) — to behold the work of the LORD at the empty tomb.
9: shadow He makes wars cease to the end of the earth; he breaks the bow, shatters the spear, and burns the chariots with fire. The eschatological peace of Christ: he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility (Ephesians 2:14). The broken bow and shattered spear are the powers disarmed at the cross (Colossians 2:15).
10: revelation of God Be still, and know that I am God. I will be exalted among the nations, I will be exalted in the earth! I am — the divine name Jesus applies to himself: before Abraham was, I am (John 8:58); I am the resurrection and the life (John 11:25). To be still and know God is to know Christ, through whom God is exalted among all nations as the Great Commission fills the earth.
11: revelation of God The LORD of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our fortress. The refrain repeated — the double assurance of divine presence. Christ's final promise: I am with you always, to the end of the age (Matthew 28:20). The LORD of hosts with us is the risen Christ present through his Spirit in the gathered church — the eternal fortress that will not be moved.
